## Binary Search Tree Insertion — Time Complexity

**Average case:** O(log n) — each comparison eliminates roughly half the remaining tree.

**Worst case:** O(n) — occurs when insertions arrive in sorted order, degenerating the tree into a linked list.

### BST Insertion Pseudocode

```
function bst_insert(root, key):
    if root is NULL:
        return new Node(key)
    if key < root.key:
        root.left = bst_insert(root.left, key)
    else if key > root.key:
        root.right = bst_insert(root.right, key)
    return root
```

---

## Red-Black Tree: How It Maintains Balance

A red-black tree is a self-balancing BST that enforces **five invariants** to guarantee O(log n) height:

| # | Invariant |
|---|-----------|
| 1 | Every node is either **red** or **black**. |
| 2 | The **root** is always black. |
| 3 | Every **NULL leaf** (sentinel) is black. |
| 4 | A **red node** cannot have a red child (no two consecutive reds). |
| 5 | Every path from a node to its descendant NULLs has the **same black-height**. |

These properties guarantee that the longest path is at most **2× the shortest path**, bounding tree height to **O(log n)**.

### Insertion Strategy

1. **Insert** the new node as in a standard BST, colored **red**.
2. **Fix violations** by walking up the tree, applying one of three cases:

### Fix-Up Pseudocode

```
function rb_insert_fixup(tree, z):
    while z.parent.color == RED:
        if z.parent == z.parent.parent.left:
            uncle = z.parent.parent.right

            // Case 1: Uncle is RED → recolor
            if uncle.color == RED:
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                uncle.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                z = z.parent.parent          // move focus up

            else:
                // Case 2: z is a right child → left-rotate to reduce to Case 3
                if z == z.parent.right:
                    z = z.parent
                    left_rotate(tree, z)

                // Case 3: z is a left child → recolor + right-rotate
                z.parent.color = BLACK
                z.parent.parent.color = RED
                right_rotate(tree, z.parent.parent)

        else:
            // Mirror: swap "left" and "right" in all of the above

    tree.root.color = BLACK                  // enforce invariant 2
```

### The Three Cases Explained

| Case | Condition | Action | Effect |
|------|-----------|--------|--------|
| **1** | Uncle is red | Recolor parent, uncle → black; grandparent → red; move up | Pushes the violation upward — may recurse O(log n) times |
| **2** | Uncle is black, z is inner child | Rotate z's parent (transforms into Case 3) | Structural realignment — 1 rotation |
| **3** | Uncle is black, z is outer child | Recolor + rotate grandparent | Restores all invariants — 1 rotation |

### Rotation Pseudocode

```
function left_rotate(tree, x):
    y = x.right
    x.right = y.left           // adopt y's left subtree
    if y.left ≠ NULL:
        y.left.parent = x
    y.parent = x.parent        // link y to x's parent
    if x.parent == NULL:
        tree.root = y
    else if x == x.parent.left:
        x.parent.left = y
    else:
        x.parent.right = y
    y.left = x                 // x becomes y's left child
    x.parent = y
```

`right_rotate` is the symmetric mirror.

### Complexity Summary

| Operation | BST (worst) | Red-Black Tree (worst) |
|-----------|-------------|----------------------|
| Insert    | O(n)        | **O(log n)** |
| Search    | O(n)        | **O(log n)** |
| Delete    | O(n)        | **O(log n)** |
| Rotations per insert | — | **At most 2** |
| Recolorings per insert | — | **O(log n)** |

The key insight: rotations are O(1) and at most 2 are needed per insertion. The recoloring loop may traverse up to the root (O(log n)), but each step is O(1) work — so the total fix-up cost is **O(log n)**.
